rows.title: Vermont HB 821: Artificial Intelligence Systems Regulation Bill
rows.summary:
Vermont House Bill 821 (HB 821) is a proposed state-level bill introduced in the Vermont General Assembly that addresses the regulation of artificial intelligence systems, with a particular focus on automated decision-making and consumer protections within the jurisdiction of the State of Vermont, United States.

On introduction in the 2023-2024 legislative session, the Vermont General Assembly considered HB 821 as a measure to establish requirements for developers and deployers of artificial intelligence systems operating within or affecting Vermont residents. The bill was referred to the relevant House committee for consideration during the session.

HB 821 proposed requirements for entities that develop or deploy AI systems used in consequential decisions affecting Vermont consumers. The bill set out provisions addressing transparency obligations, requiring that individuals subject to automated decision-making be notified of the use of such systems. The bill also proposed that consumers be afforded certain rights in relation to decisions made through AI systems, including the right to seek human review of automated determinations that affect access to services, employment, credit, housing, and similar high-stakes domains. The legislators directed attention to high-risk AI systems, which the bill defined by reference to the categories of consequential decisions they inform.

The bill further proposed that developers of AI systems conduct impact assessments prior to deployment, evaluating potential risks to consumers including risks of algorithmic discrimination. Deployers operating in Vermont would be required under the bill's terms to maintain documentation of the AI systems they use and to make certain disclosures available to the Vermont Attorney General upon request. Enforcement of the bill's provisions, if enacted, would fall to the Vermont Attorney General, with civil penalties available for violations. The bill did not specify a fixed penalty amount in the publicly circulated draft versions reviewed. No enacted version of HB 821 has been confirmed; the bill remained in the legislative process as of the most recent available information.

rows.title: Vermont HB 341: Artificial Intelligence Systems Transparency and Consumer Protection Bill
rows.summary:
Vermont House Bill 341 is a proposed state-level measure before the Vermont General Assembly that would impose transparency, accountability and consumer protection requirements on developers and deployers of artificial intelligence systems operating within the state of Vermont, United States.

On introduction in the 2025–2026 legislative session, Vermont legislators filed HB 341 in the Vermont House of Representatives. The bill forms part of a wider pattern of state-level AI governance proposals emerging across the United States in the absence of a comprehensive federal AI statute.

The bill, as introduced, targets both developers and deployers of AI systems, establishing disclosure obligations where AI is used in consequential decisions affecting Vermont consumers. These consequential decision contexts include areas such as employment, credit, housing, education and healthcare determinations. The bill requires that consumers be notified when an AI system has been used to make or materially contribute to a decision affecting them, and that a mechanism for human review of such decisions be made available. Developers of high-risk AI systems would be required to conduct and document impact assessments prior to deployment, covering accuracy, bias, data governance and risk mitigation measures.

The bill also addresses data practices associated with AI systems, requiring that personal data used in the training or operation of covered AI systems be handled in accordance with existing Vermont privacy law, including the Vermont Data Privacy Act framework. Enforcement authority under HB 341 would vest in the Vermont Attorney General, who would be empowered to investigate violations and seek civil penalties. The bill does not create a private right of action for individual consumers.

No fiscal note or committee hearing date had been published at the time of analysis, and the bill's progress through the House Committee on Commerce and Economic Development remained pending.
